skill.name: ai-image-generation
skill.description: Generate and edit images through the inference.sh CLI using GPT-Image-2, FLUX, Gemini, Grok, Seedream, Reve, and related image models.
skill.summary: Generate, edit, upscale, and stitch AI images through inference.sh image apps.
skill.author: inference-sh

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command and URL patterns plus one high weak-crypto alert. Review found the weak-crypto alert and most backtick detections are Markdown false positives, while the real risk is intended remote CLI execution that sends prompts and image URLs to inference.sh image apps.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Sends Prompts to Remote Model Services
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill documents belt commands that run remote inference.sh image apps. This is expected functionality, but prompts and job inputs leave the local environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples clearly invoke remote model app IDs through the belt CLI. The usage appears intentional and documented, not hidden or malicious.

medium_findings.title: Image URLs May Be Shared With Remote Providers
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The examples pass image URLs into remote image editing, upscaling, and stitching apps. Users could expose private images if they provide sensitive URLs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly include image URL fields sent as app input. The actual provider handling depends on inference.sh and the selected app, so the privacy risk is contextual.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs Are Benign
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The hardcoded URLs point to the inference.sh site, documentation, an installation guide, a display image, or placeholder user image URLs. No hidden download or exfiltration endpoint was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The linked destinations are visible in Markdown and align with the skill purpose. I found no evidence of obfuscated URLs or secret data transfer.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The static analyzer treated Markdown inline code and fenced shell examples as Ruby backtick execution. SKILL.md is documentation and contains no Ruby code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file content is Markdown with code formatting and bash examples. There is no Ruby runtime context or dynamic backtick evaluation in the skill package.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Alert Is False Positive
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The high-severity weak cryptography alert points to the frontmatter description. No cryptographic algorithm, hashing routine, or encryption code appears at that location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is a plain language skill description listing image models and trigger phrases. I found no evidence of cryptographic code or weak algorithm usage.

dangerous_patterns.title: External CLI Sends Prompts to Remote Model Services
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill documents belt commands that run remote inference.sh image apps. This is expected functionality, but prompts and job inputs leave the local environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples clearly invoke remote model app IDs through the belt CLI. The usage appears intentional and documented, not hidden or malicious.

dangerous_patterns.title: Image URLs May Be Shared With Remote Providers
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The examples pass image URLs into remote image editing, upscaling, and stitching apps. Users could expose private images if they provide sensitive URLs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly include image URL fields sent as app input. The actual provider handling depends on inference.sh and the selected app, so the privacy risk is contextual.

content.value_statement: Teams need quick access to many image models without learning each provider API. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code reusable prompts for running image apps through the inference.sh CLI.
actual_capabilities: Lists image generation models available through inference.sh app IDs.
Shows text-to-image prompts for GPT-Image-2, FLUX, Gemini, Grok, Seedream, and Reve.
Documents image editing workflows that accept existing image URLs.
Includes examples for image upscaling and stitching multiple images.
Helps choose models based on quality, speed, style, text rendering, or cost.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh belt CLI and an authenticated account.
Sends prompts, parameters, and image URLs to external inference services.
Does not generate images locally or offline.
Does not verify model availability, pricing, or provider policy changes.

use_cases.title: Create Concept Visuals
use_cases.description: Generate fast visual directions for products, scenes, characters, or campaigns before commissioning final artwork.

use_cases.title: Prepare Marketing Assets
use_cases.description: Produce social graphics, product mockups, posters, and background variations using model-specific prompt examples.

use_cases.title: Prototype Image Workflows
use_cases.description: Test different hosted image models from a terminal workflow before integrating a production image pipeline.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a Simple Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an image prompt for a clean product photo of a reusable water bottle on a light studio background.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Model Choices
prompt_templates.prompt: Recommend the best inference.sh image model for a cinematic landscape, a poster with text, and a low-cost draft image.

prompt_templates.title: Edit an Existing Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me prepare an image editing request that changes the background of an existing product photo to a beach at sunset.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Multi-Step Asset Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a workflow to create three ad concepts, pick the strongest result, upscale it, and stitch variants into a review board.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a product photo of sneakers with studio lighting.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends GPT-Image-2 or FLUX, drafts a concise product photography prompt, and notes quality settings to consider.

output_examples.input: A user has a poster idea that must contain readable words.
output_examples.output: The assistant suggests a text-focused model such as Reve or Seedream and rewrites the prompt to make the required lettering explicit.

output_examples.input: A user wants to improve a generated image for presentation.
output_examples.output: The assistant suggests an upscaling step, explains that the image URL will be sent to a remote service, and prepares the next action.

best_practices: Use non-sensitive prompts and image URLs unless the provider terms allow private data.
Choose the model based on the needed output, such as text rendering, quality, speed, or cost.
Review generated images for brand, licensing, safety, and accuracy before publication.
anti_patterns: Do not send confidential product images or private customer photos without approval.
Do not assume every listed model is available, priced the same, or policy compatible.
Do not rely on a single generation when production work needs comparison and review.

faq.question: Does this skill create images by itself?
faq.answer: No. It guides an assistant to use the inference.sh CLI and hosted image apps.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Does it support image editing?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes examples for editing existing images by providing image URLs to supported apps.

faq.question: Can it upscale images?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes an example that uses a Topaz image upscaling app through inference.sh.

faq.question: What are the main security considerations?
faq.answer: Prompts, parameters, and image URLs may be sent to remote services. Users should avoid sensitive content.

faq.question: Does it require setup?
faq.answer: Yes. Users need the belt CLI, an inference.sh account, and login before running app commands.
